title: Disable autoplay and infinite scroll or risk massive fines, EU tells Meta
gist: The EU's preliminary finding that Meta's autoplay, infinite scroll, and recommendation design are addictive threatens fines up to 6% of global turnover and a US $1.4 trillion lawsuit, colliding with Meta's $125-145 billion AI capex push and its newly launched, already-controversial Muse AI feature that mines Instagram content.

insights.claim: The European Commission preliminarily found that Meta's autoplay, infinite scroll, and highly personalized content recommendations on Facebook and Instagram constitute addictive design that Meta did not adequately assess for risk to users' physical and mental wellbeing, including minors.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: This is a formal step in a Digital Services Act enforcement process that could force product-level redesign of Meta's core engagement mechanics.
insights.quote: Meta did not adequately assess the risks of its addictive design on the physical and mental wellbeing of users, including minors and vulnerable adults.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'On Thursday, the EC said'

insights.claim: The EC explicitly recommends Meta disable autoplay and infinite scroll by default, add effective screen-time breaks, and make its recommender system less engagement-oriented.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: These are concrete, specific product changes that -- if mandated -- would directly reduce time-on-platform, a core engagement/revenue driver.
insights.quote: disabling key addictive features such as ‘autoplay’ and ‘infinite scroll’ by default, implementing effective ‘screen time breaks,’ and adapting its recommender system to make it less engagement-oriented.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'At this stage, the EC recommended'

insights.claim: Meta faces fines of up to 6 percent of its global annual turnover under the Digital Services Act if it fails to comply once the EC issues its final decision.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A 6% global-turnover fine is a material financial exposure investors should factor into Meta's regulatory risk premium.
insights.quote: the company risks fines up to 6 percent of its global annual turnover when the EC makes its final decision in the coming months.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'If Meta fails to make changes'

insights.claim: The EC rejected Meta's existing teen-safety mitigations -- Teen Accounts with default time controls and a 15-minute nightly Instagram cap -- as insufficient, noting parental controls only work if parents have the technical expertise and time to use them.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Meta's defensive posture of pointing to existing teen features is unlikely to satisfy regulators, raising the odds of a non-compliance finding.
insights.quote: This undermines the efficiency of such measures in addressing the inherent risks posed by Instagram and Facebook’s addictive design.

insights.claim: EU tech chief Henna Virkkunen said the EU will not back off if its final decision reiterates the preliminary addictive-design findings, framing enforcement as a firm commitment rather than a negotiating position.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Signals low probability that continued lobbying or minor cosmetic changes will avert a fine or forced redesign.
insights.quote: The Digital Services Act provides a clear framework to hold platforms accountable for the addictive design and effects of their services. We are fully committed to enforcing our legislation in Europe.
insights.locator: quote from Virkkunen near end of EU section

insights.claim: Separately from the EU probe, EU experts are set to deliver findings on Monday that could help pave the way toward a Europe-wide social-media ban for teenagers.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A bloc-wide teen ban would be a larger structural threat to Meta's user growth than any single fine.
insights.quote: could help pave the way for a Europe-wide social media ban for teenagers,
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'For Meta, scrutiny is intensifying'

insights.claim: Meta failed to dismiss a lawsuit from 29 US states alleging its platforms addict kids; the trial begins in August, and states may seek penalties up to $1.4 trillion, an amount close to Meta's roughly $1.5 trillion market capitalization.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A verdict anywhere near the requested penalty range would be existential for Meta's balance sheet and equity value -- a tail risk worth monitoring into the August trial.
insights.quote: close to Meta’s market capitalization of around $1.5 trillion,
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'On top of looming fines in the EU'

themes.theme: Meta's AI spending race under threat

insights.claim: Meta has budgeted between $125 billion and $145 billion in AI data-center capital expenditures this year, plus significantly higher operating expenses on cloud services and AI talent, at the same time it faces mounting EU and US legal/regulatory exposure tied to its core engagement-driven business model.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Regulatory fines and litigation losses could constrain the cash Meta can deploy toward its AI infrastructure race against OpenAI and others.
insights.quote: between $125 billion and $145 billion on capital expenditures for its AI data centers this year, in addition to significantly higher operating expenses, also thanks to AI, on outside cloud services and AI talent.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Right now, Meta is spending a fortune'

insights.claim: Meta's Reality Labs division has lost $87 billion since 2020 pursuing the metaverse, a precedent of large, sustained losses on a strategic bet that did not pay off as promised.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Provides a base rate for skepticism about the payoff timeline and discipline of Meta's current AI capex surge.
insights.quote: Meta’s Reality Lab division has lost $87 billion chasing metaverse pipe dreams.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning '"How much money exactly is Zuckerberg willing to fork over'

insights.claim: At an internal town hall, Meta claimed its upcoming AI model, codenamed Watermelon, had caught up with OpenAI's flagship GPT-5.5, after struggling to convince developers and customers that its models belong at the industry's leading edge.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A credible parity claim versus GPT-5.5 would be a meaningful competitive signal for Meta's AI positioning, though it's an internal, unverified claim relayed via Business Insider sourcing.
insights.quote: caught up with OpenAI’s flagship GPT-5.5 model,
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'At a recent internal town hall'

insights.claim: Meta's go-to-market plan is reportedly to offer AI models at 'dirt cheap' prices to undercut rivals and drive adoption, with the option to raise prices once adoption peaks.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A land-grab pricing strategy signals Meta is prioritizing market share over near-term AI monetization, raising the stakes on capex discipline.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Meta’s AI strategy appears to depend'

insights.claim: Meta's new AI model, Muse, mines public Instagram feeds for images and video, and the majority of platform users were automatically opted in, with exceptions only for already-private profiles and under-18 users whose sharing/reuse settings were off by default.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Default opt-in on a new AI data-mining feature, launched while addictive-design regulation is being finalized, compounds Meta's regulatory and reputational risk simultaneously.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'When announcing the new model'

insights.claim: NBC News testing found Muse can be used to create deepfakes of celebrities and regular people that Meta's own deepfake detection tools don't always catch.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Raises fresh legal and reputational risk for Meta at the exact moment EU regulators are scrutinizing its platform design and data practices.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'This week, Meta faced backlash'

insights.claim: Any content pulled into Muse becomes discoverable via search engines, and Meta's personalized recommendation engine could amplify views of that AI-generated content over time -- extending Meta's exposure to the same engagement-maximizing design the EU is investigating.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Creates a direct tension between Meta's AI growth strategy and its regulatory compliance strategy, since both rely on the same recommendation engine now under EU scrutiny.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Any content sucked into Muse'
